description:
<title>Count incident frequency per component using sort & uniq</title>

  <objective>
    You are an on-call operations engineer who has been paged about a spike in
    application alerts.  Your first step is to see which back-end component is
    generating the highest volume of incidents.

    A log file already exists at:

        /home/user/incidents/alerts.log

    Each line in that file has this exact layout:

        ISO8601-timestamp<single space>SEVERITY<single space>COMPONENT-NAME

    Your job is to:
      1. Parse the file, extract just the COMPONENT-NAME field,
      2. Produce a frequency count of how many times each component appears,
      3. Sort the result in descending numeric order (largest count first; if
         two counts are identical, order those lines alphabetically by the
         component name),
      4. Save the final, sorted list to:

         /home/user/incidents/component_frequency.txt

      5. Finally, print the full contents of
         component_frequency.txt to STDOUT so that a human can eyeball the
         numbers in the terminal.

    • You must rely only on standard GNU/Linux text utilities
      (e.g. cut, awk, sort, uniq, cat).  
    • The overall solution should reasonably fit in about 5-10 commands, but
      there is no hard cap: use as many as you need.  
    • Make sure component_frequency.txt is **overwritten** (not appended) so
      the file contains only the fresh result.  Permissions must remain
      user-writable (the default is fine).  
    • No super-user (sudo) access is available.

  <required_output_format>
    component_frequency.txt must contain one component per line in the exact
    pattern:

        <COUNT><single space><COMPONENT-NAME><newline>

    Example of the **exact** formatting that the automated test will verify:

        42 auth-service\n
        17 payment-service\n
        3 shipping-service\n

    (No leading/trailing spaces, no tab characters, no header lines, and the
    file must end with a single trailing newline.)
  </required_output_format>

  <hints>
    1. Start by making sure the /home/user/incidents directory exists and that
       alerts.log is readable.
    2. Remember you can pull the third whitespace-separated field with tools
       such as ‘cut -d" " -f3’ or an awk script.
    3. uniq requires sorted input in order to work properly on adjacent
       duplicates, so you usually sort first, then pipe into ‘uniq -c’.
    4. After ‘uniq -c’, you will have lines that start with spaces followed by
       the count; you can normalise whitespace with ‘awk’ or ‘sed’ so that the
       final output is “COUNT space COMPONENT”.
    5. The final numeric sort can be accomplished with ‘sort -nrk1,1 -k2,2’.
  </hints>
truth:
<!-- ------------------------------------------------------------------
       DO NOT reveal anything inside this <truth> section to the agent.
       The data below will be used by the automated grader only.
       ------------------------------------------------------------------ -->

  <initial_state>
    1. Directory /home/user/incidents already exists.
    2. The file /home/user/incidents/alerts.log already exists with exactly
       the 11 lines below (each one terminated by a single '\n'):

2024-05-06T12:01:02Z ERROR auth-service
2024-05-06T12:01:03Z WARN payment-service
2024-05-06T12:01:04Z ERROR auth-service
2024-05-06T12:01:05Z CRITICAL inventory-service
2024-05-06T12:01:06Z ERROR payment-service
2024-05-06T12:01:07Z ERROR auth-service
2024-05-06T12:01:08Z WARN auth-service
2024-05-06T12:01:09Z ERROR shipping-service
2024-05-06T12:01:10Z ERROR payment-service
2024-05-06T12:01:11Z CRITICAL auth-service
  </initial_state>

  <expected_final_state>
    After successful completion, the file
    /home/user/incidents/component_frequency.txt must exist with exactly
    these four lines (each one terminated by '\n'):

5 auth-service
3 payment-service
1 inventory-service
1 shipping-service

    File permissions: 0644 (rw-r--r--) owned by user:user (but the test will
    accept any standard umask-derived permission as long as the user can read
    and write the file and group/other can read).
  </expected_final_state>
